content:
4 min read
Just now

--

I had a knowledge base that cleaned itself. Every morning, while I slept, a job read the new research and notes I’d accumulated across a lot of separate project areas, pulled out the durable claims, and filed them as structured “hypotheses” I could review later. It ran on schedule and handed me a fresh review queue every morning. By every outward sign it was working.
It was also drowning me, and it took me embarrassingly long to understand why.
The symptom
Every week or so I’d open the review queue and start archiving entries that were obviously redundant — the same claim, slightly reworded, that I’d archived the week before. I’d clear a batch, feel a little tidier, and move on. The next week the queue would be full again with the same kind of thing.
I told myself this was normal. A self-populating system generates some noise; you prune it; that’s the deal. What I didn’t notice, because the regeneration happened overnight on a different machine while I wasn’t watching, was that I wasn’t pruning a growing garden. I was mowing the same patch of lawn over and over and congratulating myself each time on how short the grass was.
The number, when I finally counted, was 1,124 open entries. That felt like a lot for the amount of genuinely new thinking I’d done. So I stopped clearing the queue, and started asking where the entries were coming from.
The diagnosis
I traced each entry back to the source file it had been extracted from. The pattern was immediate and ugly.
A huge share of the noise traced back to one kind of file: my own canonical reference documents. The curated, authoritative docs I’d written to be the single source of truth for each area. In one area, that single file was responsible for almost half the open entries. In another, well over half. Same shape everywhere I looked.
Of course it does. The extractor’s job is to read source material and pull out durable claims. I had pointed it at documents that were nothing but durable claims, already curated by me. So every time I edited one of those reference docs — improving it, which I did often — the extractor dutifully re-read it and produced a fresh batch of “new” claims that were just lower-fidelity paraphrases of statements that were already authoritative in the source. I was asking a model to summarize my summaries, and then filing the summaries-of-summaries as discoveries.
The wrong fix I almost shipped
My first instinct was to automate the mowing. Write a job that detects entries sourced from those reference docs and auto-archives them on a schedule. I had it half-drafted.
It would have made the queue look clean while leaving the source of the noise untouched. I’d have stopped noticing the problem entirely, while the same noise kept regenerating every night and the archive quietly bloated forever. The grass would be short and still growing, because I’d never touched the thing that grows it.
The actual fix was to ask why those documents were being read at all. And the answer was: they shouldn’t be. They’re not inputs to the pipeline. They’re the output — the curated reference the whole extraction process exists to help me build. Feeding them back in as raw material was the loop, not a cleanup problem.
The one line
The fix was smaller than the cleanup I’d almost written. Every area’s canonical document follows the same naming convention, so a single skip rule in the enumeration step catches all of them at once:
SKIP_FILENAMES = {“skill.md”} # canonical reference docs: output, not input
# … in the file-enumeration loop:
if fname.lower() in SKIP_FILENAMES:
manifest[fname] = “SKIP_CANONICAL”
continue
One line of actual logic, plus the skip-list it reads from. (Simplified from the actual loop for clarity.) Then I deleted the backlog of noise it had already generated in one historical sweep, and the open queue dropped from 1,124 entries to 566 — call it cut roughly in half, overnight. The sweep is what removed the backlog. The skip rule is the part that matters more than the number, because it means that particular loop can’t regenerate. I didn’t clear the queue faster; I removed the thing that was filling it.
The general version
The specific bug is narrow. The principle isn’t: a canonical source of truth shouldn’t be treated as raw material by the process that produces it. It’s the destination, not the feedstock. If I feed the finished reference doc back into the extractor, I’m not discovering anything. I’m asking the system to paraphrase me.
The cleanest example outside my own setup: asking a model to rediscover the insights in a strategy document you wrote yourself last week. It’ll hand you back your own thinking as a list of “findings.” They sound new. They’re blurrier copies of what you already had.
So now, before any document becomes a source for an automated step, I ask one thing: did a process of mine produce this, or did the world hand it to me raw? If it’s the polished, authoritative artifact — the place I go to find the truth rather than generate it — it gets skipped. It earned its place by being the answer.
Author note: I’m Fillip Kosorukov. PubMed indexes one of my behavioral-psychology papers under PMID 37275205.
My ORCID record is https://orcid.org/0009-0004-1430-2859. For software notes, I keep the home base at https://fillipkosorukov.net/.
